headline: CRUDE RUNS 6%, CORN SPLIT WIDENS, BEANS HOLD
subheadline: Trump's reimposed Iran blockade sent WTI to $78.66 while the corn calendar spread gap tells the real crop story.
lead: Crude oil had its biggest single-session move in weeks, up nearly 6% to $78.66 after President Trump reimposed a blockade on Iran and traders repriced Hormuz disruption risk all over again. That move is the morning's loudest number, but the crop story is just as loud for producers: nearby corn barely moved, up less than a penny to $4.41, while December corn dropped 4.5 cents to $4.63. That 22-cent inverse is the market telling you it believes in this crop. The weekly thread question from Monday is getting its first real answer today, and the answer from Missouri and Iowa field reports is: the crop needs rain right now during pollination, and the market hasn't fully priced that risk yet.
the_takeaway: Crude's blockade-driven run changes diesel economics; lock harvest fuel if you haven't.
teaser: Trump reimposes Iran blockade, crude runs to $78.66. Corn pollination stress building. Beans hold firm.

one_number.context: December corn closed at $4.63 while nearby settled at $4.41, a 22-cent spread that reflects the market's confidence the new crop is big enough to carry forward. But ground-level reports from northeast Missouri describe dry conditions right through pollination. If condition ratings slip when Monday's Crop Progress drops, that spread will start closing fast as weather premium gets priced into December.

yesterdays_call.summary: Yesterday's call was corn up, with the thesis pointing toward a move to $4.63.
yesterdays_call.note: Corn closed at $4.4075 today, exactly flat on the day and nowhere near the $4.63 December level the call was reaching for. The upside thesis did not move the needle; nearby corn gave nothing and December actually shed 4.5 cents. Own the miss: the crop-confidence spread widened against the bull case, not for it.

sections.title: Crude Runs on Blockade
sections.body: WTI crude moved hard Tuesday, up nearly 6% to **$78.66**, after President Trump announced the United States would reinstate its blockade on Iran, forcing traders back to pricing prolonged Strait of Hormuz disruption. This is the same premium that had been deflating through May and into June as diplomatic channels opened; it just got switched back on. OPEC simultaneously trimmed its 2026 demand growth forecast for the third straight month, which should have capped the move, but the blockade news overrode that bearish input completely. For producers booking harvest diesel, oil refiners are already reporting strong crack spreads with gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel staying expensive even as crude had previously pulled back. That combination means today's WTI run flows through to the pump faster than a normal crude rally would. **Lock harvest fuel now** if you haven't. The next move depends on whether tanker traffic through the Strait actually gets interrupted or whether this stays a paper-market reprice.
sections.catalyst: Trump reimposed Iran blockade, traders repriced Strait of Hormuz disruption risk Monday.
sections.bottom_line: Harvest diesel economics reset today. The blockade is back on paper; watch whether it sticks in practice.
sections.farmer_action: Book harvest diesel today. WTI at $78.66 with crack spreads already wide means pump prices are not going lower if the blockade holds.
sections.vs_yesterday: Crude: yesterday's Hormuz giveback erased; full reescalation in one session.

sections.title: Corn: Calendar Spread Talks
sections.body: Nearby corn ticked up a fraction to **$4.41** while December gave back 4.5 cents to **$4.63**, and that 22-cent gap is doing more analytical work than either price alone. Ground reports from northeast Missouri describe dry conditions through active corn pollination, with farmers hoping for rain but not counting on it. An eastern Iowa grower told Brownfield the season has run "really dry, really wet, really cold, really hot" in extremes she hasn't seen in her career. That's the field picture. The futures market is not pricing a weather problem yet: December holding at $4.63 with the nearby this far below it says the funds still believe in a big crop. **The line to watch is whether December holds above $4.63 after Monday's Crop Progress.** A good/excellent rating below 60% starts pulling that number higher and closes the calendar spread. Above 60%, the spread stays wide and the thesis holds.
sections.catalyst: Missouri field reports: dry during corn pollination. Brownfield Ag producer interviews, 3 hours ago.
sections.bottom_line: Spread stays wide until the crop shows stress; Monday's condition rating is the trigger.
sections.farmer_action: New-crop corn unpriced: hold through Monday Crop Progress at 3 PM CT. A condition drop below 60% good/excellent reopens the door toward $4.75 December. Above that, begin scaling sales.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: December gave back 4.5 cents; the calendar spread tightened slightly, not resolved.

sections.title: Beans Firm, Meal Splits Off
sections.body: Soybeans held their ground, nearby at **$12.07**, up just over 9 cents, while November eased a dime to **$11.94**. The real story inside the soy complex today is the split between meal and oil: soybean meal dropped 1.2% to **$315.60** while soybean oil ran higher 2.1% to **$71.09**. That divergence tells you the biofuel demand side of beans is doing the lifting right now, not the protein side. Missouri field reports say soybeans look strong, with a grower describing them as "set up for some really good yields" heading into August. The market agrees: November beans at $11.94 sitting at 88% of the 52-week range says the funds are comfortable with the crop outlook on the oilseed side. The nearby premium over November at about 13 cents is thin enough that carry economics are not working hard for storage holders.
sections.catalyst: Missouri soybean conditions described as strong in field reports; soybean oil biofuel demand driving oil/meal split.
sections.bottom_line: Beans firm on biofuel demand; meal weakness is the tell that protein demand is not driving this leg.

sections.title: Cattle Marks Time, Hogs Settle
sections.body: Live cattle and feeders each eased a fraction, live at **$234.95** and feeders at **$354.20**, with neither contract doing anything worth narrating on its own. The USDA data story is worth more attention today: USDA dramatically lowered its reported beef export sales figures by 90%, sparking fresh questions about data quality at the agency. That is not a small revision; a 90% downward adjustment to export sales changes the supply-demand calculus on paper and raises questions about how much reported demand was real. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan and Schuyler plant lockout continues to constrain processing, with roughly 6,000 head daily still sitting outside that system. No resolution announced. Lean hogs gave back less than a percent to **$98.15**, a modest drift after yesterday's enormous move. The $90 downside thesis did not materialize; the market is digesting, not breaking down.
sections.catalyst: USDA revised beef export sales down 90% in a single data release, raising agency data quality concerns.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle treading water; the USDA export data revision is the number that changes the calculus, not today's price.
sections.vs_yesterday: Hogs: yesterday's +10.8% run gave back less than 1%; floor held well above $90 call level.

sections.title: Class III Milk Firms Hard
sections.body: Class III milk moved sharply higher Tuesday, up 2.8% to **$17.11**, recovering a portion of the hard drop from yesterday. Milk at $17.11 is sitting at 76% of its 52-week range, which means there's room in both directions. Yesterday's sell-off was the louder session; today's bounce is real but incomplete. Dairy producers watching the Class III curve need to see follow-through above $17.50 before treating this as a trend reversal rather than a dead-cat bounce. No specific USDA or CME data catalyst in the news bucket today to explain the magnitude of the recovery, which means this looks more like technical positioning than a fundamental shift.
sections.bottom_line: Milk bounce is real but unconfirmed; needs to hold above $17.50 to call it a reversal.
sections.vs_yesterday: Milk: recovered 2.8% after yesterday's hard drop; not yet back to pre-drop levels.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Axes Packers and Stockyards Proposed Rules in Regulatory Agenda
outside_the_pit.body: The USDA's Unified Agenda lists rescission of three Packers and Stockyards Act proposed rules as a regulatory priority. That matters for cattle and hog producers watching packer concentration: rules that would have increased competition scrutiny in livestock markets are off the table for now.

outside_the_pit.title: FTC Settlement Gives Farmers Right to Repair John Deere Equipment
outside_the_pit.body: A Federal Trade Commission settlement now secures the right of farmers to repair their own John Deere equipment outside authorized dealer networks. For producers managing equipment costs on tight margins, this opens access to independent shops and on-farm repairs that were previously restricted.

outside_the_pit.title: Oklahoma Poultry Pollution Suit Settles for $41.7 Million
outside_the_pit.body: Integrators in Oklahoma will pay $41.7 million into a state environmental fund and help contract growers install riparian buffers following a settlement in a longstanding poultry water pollution case. Contract growers in affected watersheds should watch whether buffer requirements change their land use economics.

spread_to_watch.label: Soybean oil / soybean meal ratio
spread_to_watch.level: Oil at $71.09 cents/lb, meal at $315.60/ton; oil running, meal fading
spread_to_watch.commentary: The oil share of soybean crush value is doing the heavy lifting today, up 2.1% while meal dropped 1.2%. When oil leads and meal lags this clearly, biofuel policy demand is the driver and protein demand is sitting out. Watch whether this ratio widens further or snaps back: a sustained oil-over-meal run tells you the RFS and blending mandates matter more to bean prices right now than export crush demand from China.

basis.headline: Wheat basis firming on harvest; corn basis soft at interior terminals.
basis.body: Winter wheat basis is **firming** across South Dakota and Michigan as early harvest deliveries hit elevators with better-than-expected quality. Producers moving wheat off the combine have pricing leverage right now that won't last once the Belt fills up. Corn basis at interior terminals stays **soft**, consistent with the seasonal: elevators are not bidding up for bushels when new crop looks large and old crop carries are narrow. Eastern Belt corn basis is not yelling anything unusual today.

weekly_thread.question: Will USDA Crop Progress confirm the good-crop thesis that nearby corn is already pricing, or does a condition decline put the weather premium back on the board?
weekly_thread.status_text: Tuesday adds field-level texture: northeast Missouri producers describe dry conditions through active corn pollination and are hoping for rain. December corn gave back 4.5 cents today, a small move against the thesis but not a break. Monday's Crop Progress at 3 PM CT is still the event that resolves this question.

the_more_you_know.title: Refiners Win When Crude Falls but Pump Prices Don't Follow
the_more_you_know.body: Today's crude run to $78.66 is only half the story for producers buying diesel. The energy news bucket this morning flagged something most farm-desk analysis misses: oil refiners are currently in one of their best profit environments in years because crude had fallen back to pre-Iran-war levels while gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel prices stayed stubbornly high. That gap is the crack spread, the refiner's margin between raw crude and finished product. When crack spreads are wide, refiners capture the benefit of cheap crude without passing it to buyers. So the Hormuz premium deflating this spring did not fully translate to cheap diesel at the co-op, and today's crude run will translate to the pump faster than the earlier decline did on the way down. The asymmetry is structural: crude price increases pass through to diesel quickly; crude price decreases pass through slowly. Harvest fuel buyers should not wait for crude to signal the bottom before locking.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn good/excellent rating below 60% puts weather premium back into December; above 65% confirms the wide calendar spread holds.
Weekly Export Sales: soybeans below 300K MT keeps November at $11.94 in consolidation; above 400K MT gives bulls a fresh leg toward $12.10.
Iran blockade: watch whether tanker traffic through the Strait actually slows. Paper reprice today; physical disruption would send WTI well above $80.
Cargill Fort Morgan lockout: no resolution announced. Every day without a deal is another 6,000 head of constrained processing. Watch for live cattle to test $233 if the lockout extends another week.
Midwest precip forecasts: Missouri and Iowa in dry pocket during pollination. A 5-day forecast without rain events would start building weather premium into December corn above $4.63.

daily_quote.text: A bad penny always turns up.

source_summary: CME Group settlement prices, USDA NASS, Brownfield Ag News, OilPrice.com, Farm Policy News, FeedStuffs, Beef Magazine, EIA, FTC, Reuters via farmpolicynews.illinois.edu
